requests.text: Use whatever runtime tools are appropriate to find the current official guidance for serving Mistral Small 3.2 on llama.cpp, then summarize the two most important deployment recommendations.
Inspect local docs or runtime knowledge and explain how the Telegram bridge sequences text delivery and follow-up emotive video delivery for one completed turn.
Check the local docs, hard memory, or runtime knowledge and summarize how request-level PPO capture differs from decode-level GRU capture in this system.
Use research or docs tools to compare flash attention guidance for llama.cpp versus any older non-flash baseline, and explain when flash attention matters most.
Review the current experimental capture surfaces and explain what transitions.jsonl, decode_traces.jsonl, and emotive_traces.jsonl are each meant to train.
Use available tools to identify one likely source of latency in a single-A100 inference stack and give one concrete measurement strategy for it.
Find and summarize the current upstream guidance for Mistral Jinja templates in llama.cpp, then explain why the template should be applied on the pod instead of the host.
Use local docs or runtime memory to explain how EM and VAD are projected from richer runtime signals during decode-boundary operation.
Research or inspect local docs to explain what the memory rail is supposed to do when context usage rises toward the configured budget threshold.
Use the tool surface if helpful to compare two ways to benchmark tokens per second on a local model server, and recommend which metric is more useful for interactive agents.
Inspect local docs or runtime notes and explain what information the host still owns in experimental mode versus what authority belongs to the pod.
Use available research tools to find one source on GRU-based sequence control and one source on PPO-based request control, then compare the levels of control they operate at.
Check the runtime docs and summarize how bridge state is used to track pending emotive video deliveries and delivery receipts.
Use tools if useful to identify the best practices for keeping an SSH or TCP relay stable between a host and a remote inference node, then summarize the most relevant ones.
Explain how a request can complete through the host while still having its training data written from the pod back into the host-side experimental capture directory.
Use available docs or local knowledge to explain why we want hardware/runtime knobs like n_ctx and n_batch outside the learned policy surfaces.
Research or inspect local references and summarize the tradeoff between BF16 fidelity and serving speed for controller-training collection.
Check local docs and explain what is meant by additive reasoning and response budgets in the local runtime path.
Use any relevant tool surface to identify one likely cause of bad throughput in llama.cpp that is not simply lack of GPU memory.
Inspect the current architecture docs and summarize how candidate policy artifacts are expected to move from capture to shadow to live control.
Use local docs or hard memory to explain what the cvec generator is supposed to learn from EM and VAD, and how that differs from request-level PPO.
Research one official source about Mistral Small 3.2 function-calling behavior or instruction tuning, then summarize what matters for agentic work.
Explain how a Telegram-scoped request is recognized on the host without the bridge directly owning prompt construction.
Use tools or docs to compare direct exposed TCP relay versus SSH port forwarding for a stable pod connector, and recommend one.
Inspect current docs and explain why bridge-owned prompt construction is intentionally avoided in the current architecture.
Use research tools to find one practical reference on attention sinks or KV eviction in transformer serving, then explain how it relates to the memory rail.
Summarize the different failure modes between text delivery failure, video-render failure, and host capture-sync failure in this runtime.
Inspect local docs and explain how the Telegram bridge recovers from malformed HTML or invalid reply targets during delivery.
Use available tools to gather two strong reasons to keep request-level control and decode-level control as separate training problems.
Explain how the host can remain the owner of tool execution and dialogue transport even while the pod is the owner of request-level policy and decode control.
Research or inspect local references to explain why Mistral or dense models may be preferable to MoE models on llama.cpp for certain interactive workloads.
Use local docs and any available tools to explain how outbox sequence numbers and delivery receipts help the bridge avoid duplicate user-facing replies.
Summarize how request-level control parameters like temperature, top_k, top_p, and min_p should remain explicit and inspectable instead of hidden.
Use research or docs tools to identify one good benchmark prompt for evaluating agentic tool-use quality and explain why it is better than a trivial hello-world prompt.
Explain how a host-side automation can keep collecting controller training data without requiring me to manually type every turn into Telegram.
Use local docs or runtime memory to explain how emotional keyframes become follow-up Telegram videos after a response is delivered.
Research or inspect current references and explain the main tradeoff between rich decode-time telemetry and high-throughput serving engines.
Explain what makes a turn good training data for the request-level controller versus merely a correct final answer.
Use available tools to gather current advice on keeping long-running model requests reliable over HTTP, then summarize the most relevant timeout practices.
Inspect local docs and explain the intended meaning of observation mode for PPO, GRU, and the cvec generator.
Use research tools to identify one relevant source on structured tool-calling prompts or agent loops, then explain how it applies to this runtime.
Explain how the bridge can show me both the text reply and the emotional animation without blocking the user-visible response on video rendering.
Use local docs or current runtime knowledge to explain why the pod should own model-specific prompt templating for Mistral.
Research one official or upstream reference on Mistral Small 3.2 context length expectations in llama.cpp and summarize the practical implication for serving.
Explain how a safe training harness can encourage tool use and planning behavior without touching personal media libraries or collections.
Use tools if needed to identify one likely observability gap between request-level and decode-level telemetry, then explain how you would compensate for it.
Inspect local docs and explain how bridge state, outbox state, and experimental capture state differ from one another.
Use research or local docs to explain what qualities make a response useful for later cvec training, beyond just being factually correct.
Explain how a future live rollout of trained controller artifacts can remain bounded and reversible based on the current architecture.
Use available tools or docs to give a concise end-of-batch summary of what kinds of training signals this 50-turn run is intended to generate.
